questions.question: What is the core noisy-neighbor problem in a multi-tenant shard pool?
options: One tenant can consume disproportionate shared resources and degrade nearby tenants.
Every tenant always receives perfectly equal performance automatically.
Tenant isolation only matters for billing, not system behavior.
Sharding by tenant removes all fairness concerns by definition.
questions.explanation: Noisy neighbors are about unequal consumption in a shared failure domain.

questions.question: Why is sharding by tenant ID alone often insufficient for isolation?
options: A single tenant can still be too large or too bursty for the shard that owns it.
Tenant IDs cannot be stored in distributed databases.
It guarantees every background job is already rate-limited.
It makes quotas impossible to implement.
questions.explanation: Routing by tenant helps locality, but not necessarily fairness or capacity safety.

questions.question: What is the strongest first layer of tenant isolation in a shared system?
options: Per-tenant accounting and fair-share controls on the resources that actually bottleneck.
Immediate dedicated hardware for every customer on day one.
Removing tenant identifiers from metrics to reduce cardinality.
Assuming all enterprise tenants behave like the median.
questions.explanation: You cannot isolate what you cannot measure or limit.

questions.question: What is the main trade-off of moving a heavy tenant to a dedicated pool?
options: You gain strong neighbor protection, but pay more in capacity and migration overhead.
You guarantee perfect utilization across the fleet.
You eliminate the need for quotas on every remaining pool.
You make cross-tenant observability unnecessary.
questions.explanation: Dedicated placement is powerful, but it costs money and operational effort.

questions.question: Which metric best reveals whether one tenant is harming others on a shard?
options: Per-tenant resource share correlated with neighbor latency or error spikes on the same shard.
The number of pages in the architecture document.
The total count of source files in the service.
The average length of customer names.
questions.explanation: Noisy-neighbor diagnosis needs both attribution and observed impact on neighbors.

questions.question: Why is request-QPS-only fairness often incomplete?
options: Background jobs, compaction, and storage-heavy work can still starve neighbors even if request rates look fair.
QPS always measures CPU, IO, and memory perfectly.
Every tenant workload has identical resource shape.
Asynchronous systems never share bottlenecks.
questions.explanation: Fairness must account for the resources that really create contention.

questions.question: When is a tiered shard-class strategy usually better than one universal pool?
options: When tenant sizes and SLO expectations differ enough that one placement policy fits poorly.
When the engineering team wants to avoid any future migration logic.
When all tenants are truly identical and tiny.
When observability storage is unavailable.
questions.explanation: Tiered pools can preserve cost efficiency while giving heavy tenants stronger isolation.

questions.question: What makes a senior answer stronger on noisy neighbors?
options: It links product tiering, fairness controls, placement, and incident detection into one policy.
It claims that horizontal scaling removes tenant interference automatically.
It focuses only on database vendor names.
It avoids any economic discussion about isolation choices.
questions.explanation: Isolation is a combined technical and product policy decision, not just a routing detail.
